rules.statement: Fix the pain point manually first before automating; understand the process end-to-end
rules.failure_mode_if_violated: Building automation for a process you do not fully understand leads to brittle systems, missed edge cases, and solutions that fail in production because the underlying workflow was never validated manually.

rules.statement: Ask people to pay before building fully; 'If I build this, will you pay?' validates demand
rules.failure_mode_if_violated: Building a complete product without payment validation leads to wasted months of development on features nobody will pay for, resulting in sunk cost and a product with no market.

rules.statement: Clients buy business outcomes, not automations; explain money saved/earned, not technical complexity
rules.failure_mode_if_violated: Selling technical features instead of outcomes confuses non-technical buyers, lengthens sales cycles, and positions you as a commodity vendor rather than a strategic partner.

rules.statement: Start with an irresistible offer (even free/low cost) to validate and refine the solution
rules.failure_mode_if_violated: Launching at full price without validation means zero feedback loops, no social proof, and high friction that prevents you from learning what the market actually needs.

rules.statement: The pain point must be painful enough for people to pay; if not in pain, they will not pay
rules.failure_mode_if_violated: Targeting mild inconveniences instead of acute pain produces solutions people acknowledge as 'nice to have' but never purchase, resulting in low conversion rates and stalled revenue.

rules.statement: Be honest if automation is impossible for a client's problem; do not overpromise
rules.failure_mode_if_violated: Overpromising on automation capabilities leads to failed deliveries, refund demands, reputation damage, and potential legal liability when the solution cannot deliver what was sold.

rules.statement: Do not mention 'AI' when selling; it is becoming tacky — focus on the business outcome
rules.failure_mode_if_violated: Leading with 'AI' triggers buyer skepticism due to market saturation of AI buzzwords, positions you alongside low-quality competitors, and shifts the conversation away from the value proposition.

rules.statement: As a founder, you must market your product — being 'basically done' means nothing without distribution
rules.failure_mode_if_violated: A completed product with no distribution strategy dies in obscurity; the graveyard of startups is filled with technically excellent products that nobody ever heard about.

rules.statement: Manually onboard the first 10 users before opening broadly
rules.failure_mode_if_violated: Skipping manual onboarding means you miss critical UX friction points, fail to build early champion relationships, and scale a broken experience that drives churn.

rules.statement: The moat is specialized industry knowledge, not tool proficiency — everyone can 'build AI solutions' now
rules.failure_mode_if_violated: Competing on tool proficiency alone puts you in a race to the bottom where every new no-code builder erodes your differentiation, leaving you with no defensible position.

rules.statement: If something is hard to do, it is usually valuable; as tools get easier, value shifts to domain knowledge
rules.failure_mode_if_violated: Chasing easy-to-build solutions commoditizes your offering instantly; when everyone can replicate what you built in a weekend, margins collapse to zero.

rules.statement: What cost $50,000 last year now costs a few thousand; AI compression makes execution cheap, strategy expensive
rules.failure_mode_if_violated: Pricing based on execution effort rather than strategic value leaves money on the table as build costs plummet; clients will comparison-shop your labor against increasingly cheaper alternatives.

heuristics.condition: IF you have worked in a specific industry
heuristics.action: THEN you already have specialized knowledge others lack — start there
heuristics.reason: Domain expertise accumulated through years of industry work is the hardest asset to replicate and provides immediate credibility, insider language, and understanding of real pain points that outsiders cannot fake.

heuristics.condition: IF a client does not know what they want
heuristics.action: THEN listen first, identify the biggest pain point, start with that one problem
heuristics.reason: Clients often present scattered wishlists; focusing on the single most painful problem delivers fast, visible wins that build trust and open the door to larger engagements.

heuristics.condition: IF tutorials teach generic workflows but you never apply them
heuristics.reason: Tutorial consumption without application creates an illusion of competence; real skill only develops through building, failing, and iterating on actual projects with real constraints.

heuristics.condition: IF the process becomes repeatable across clients
heuristics.action: THEN that is the signal to start automating
heuristics.reason: Premature automation locks in assumptions that may be wrong; repeatability across multiple clients confirms the workflow is stable enough to encode into software without constant rework.

heuristics.condition: IF a client has technically savvy team members
heuristics.action: THEN get them as champions early to push adoption
heuristics.reason: Internal champions reduce resistance to new tools, provide real-time feedback during rollout, and advocate for continued investment when budget reviews occur.

heuristics.condition: IF a business lacks a technical team
heuristics.action: THEN set up a retainer model to maintain the system
heuristics.reason: Non-technical teams cannot troubleshoot integrations, API changes, or prompt drift; a retainer ensures the system stays functional and creates predictable recurring revenue.

frameworks.name: Pain Point Discovery Process
steps: Look at your industry experience and identify domains where you have deep knowledge
Understand the pain points within those domains from firsthand experience
Know a framework or methodology that can address those pain points
Talk to people in the industry to validate that the pain is real and widespread
Find the specific pain worth paying for — the one that costs money, time, or both

frameworks.name: Service-to-SaaS Progression
steps: Find a painful problem that businesses actively struggle with
Validate the problem by asking potential clients if they would pay for a solution
Build a scrappy, manual or semi-automated version to solve it
Get the first paying client and deliver results
Refine the solution based on real client feedback and edge cases
Automate the repeatable parts of the workflow
Add a user interface so clients can self-serve
Turn the solution into standalone software (SaaS)
Hire to scale operations, support, and sales

frameworks.name: Product Brief to Code Pipeline
steps: Write a detailed product brief describing the problem, target user, and desired outcome
Create a CTO system prompt that encodes architecture decisions and technical constraints
Generate a roadmap breaking the product into milestones and deliverables
Break milestones into task files with clear acceptance criteria
Use plan mode to review and validate the approach before writing code
Execute the tasks, iterating through build-test-refine cycles

frameworks.name: Technology Adoption Lifecycle for AI
steps: Recognize that AI tooling is crossing the chasm from early adopters to early majority
Understand that early adopter phase rewarded technical skill (hard to build = valuable)
Recognize that early majority phase commoditizes tools (everyone can build now)
Shift competitive advantage from tool proficiency to specialized domain knowledge
Build moats through industry expertise, proprietary data, and deep process understanding

frameworks.name: AI Agency Sales Framework
steps: Identify that clients care about exactly two variables: making more money and saving time
Calculate the ROI in terms of additional revenue generated (leads x lead value)
Calculate the ROI in terms of time saved (cost per task x frequency of task)
Frame your fee as a fraction of the total value delivered
Present the math clearly so the decision becomes obvious

frameworks.name: Unscalable-First Learning Pattern
steps: Build the first version using low-code tools like N8N and Google Sheets
Force yourself to understand every API call, webhook, and data transformation
Encounter and document every error and edge case in the manual process
Only move to scalable infrastructure after the workflow is proven and repeatable
Use the manual experience to write better specifications for the automated version

frameworks.name: Operator-to-Owner Transformation
steps: Recognize when you are stuck in operator mode (firefighting, doing everything manually)
Build dashboards that give visibility into key metrics without manual checking
Identify opportunities by analyzing patterns in the data rather than reacting to crises
Design workflows that run without your direct involvement
Shift focus to strategic outcomes — growth, positioning, and long-term value creation

metrics.name: Productivity gains from Claude Code business implementations
Full SaaS app build time with Stripe payments using Claude Code

monetization_patterns.name: Service-first validation
monetization_patterns.description: Start by selling the outcome as a service (manual or semi-automated) before investing in product development. This validates willingness to pay and reveals the exact workflow to automate.

monetization_patterns.name: ROI-anchored pricing
monetization_patterns.description: Price based on a fraction of the measurable business outcome (revenue generated or costs saved), not on hours worked or technical complexity. The client sees a clear return on investment.

monetization_patterns.name: Retainer for non-technical clients
monetization_patterns.description: For businesses without technical teams, offer a monthly retainer to maintain, monitor, and update the AI system. This creates recurring revenue and ensures the system continues to function.

monetization_patterns.name: Irresistible entry offer to SaaS upsell
monetization_patterns.description: Start with a free or low-cost offer to acquire users and validate the solution. Once the value is proven and the workflow is repeatable, transition to a SaaS subscription model.
